content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 · SACRAMENTO, 95804 · · · 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95[?]

Technical Assistance · · · Administration · · · Executive Legal · · · Enforcement · · · Conflict of Inter . . .
(916) 322-5662        322-5660              322-5601          322-6441         322-6444

November 9, 1979


C. Edward Dilkes                                                A-79-130
Telanoff, Wallin, Kress
& Dilkes
11340 W. Olympic Blvd., Ste. 145
Los Angeles, CA  90064

Dear Mr. Dilkes:

     I have been asked to respond to your letter to Mr. Stern
dated October 20, 1979. For some time we have been aware of
the conceptual difficulties of applying the provisions of the
Political Reform Act to consultants hired by state and local
government agencies. We currently have a legal intern studying
this problem, and when his work is completed we hope to be
able to develop a more satisfactory solution than the one
currently embodied in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700 (a)(2).
I have sent a copy of your letter to our intern, and as you
have obviously given a great deal of valuable thought to this
problem, we will be back in contact with you when we prepare
to take this matter to the Commission.

     In the meantime, I hope the following comments may be of
some help in answering the two specific disclosure questions
you raised in your letter. In those questions, you asked
what disclosure requirements are applicable to consultants
who: (1) advise a local entity on a bond issue while simultan-
eously performing engineering or planning consulting services for the
same entity; or (2) give financial consulting services for
a bond issue and thereafter bid on the issue. Because the
disclosure requirements applicable to almost all Government
officials are those contained in their agencies' Conflict of
Interest Codes, your question is whether such consultants
should be designated and if so what their disclosure categories
should be. In answer, I can only describe the procedures
that we use to answer such questions in Codes for which we
are the code reviewing body.

Mr. C. Edward Dilkes                                      Page Two
November 9, 1979


     Because of the difficulty of predicting what kinds of
consultants an agency may hire in the future, we generally
make the position of "Consultant" a designated one and assign
it to the same disclosure categories as the head of the
agency, i.e., we initially require the broadest possible
disclosure of all consultants.1/ We also provide, however,
that in the case of any particular consultant, the head of
the agency may make a written finding that the duties of the
consultant are limited in scope and that the consultant's
disclosure obligations should consequently be narrowed.
Such a finding must include a written description of the
consultant's duties and based on that description, a statement
of the extent of the disclosure requirements which should be
applicable to the consultant, and this finding must be for-
warded to us as code reviewing body. In effect, therefore,
the agency and the code reviewing body are determining the
disclosure categories on a consultant by consultant basis.

     When faced with the question of what the appropriate
disclosure category is for a particular consultant, we use
the same standards that we use for any designated employee.
We try to predict what kinds of financial interests would
foreseeably be affected materially by decisions in which the
consultant will be participating. Because land use planning
decisions will have a significant impact on a broad spectrum
of financial interests, we usually assign employees partici-
pating in such decisions to comprehensive disclosure categories
including all investments, interests in real property and
sources of income in the jurisdiction.2/ An employee or
consultant who was assigned to such a category and who assumed
additional duties of advising an entity on a bond issue
would not need to have his or her disclosure expanded, as it
would already be at a maximum level. On the other hand,
depending on the scope of his or her work, an employee or
consultant providing engineering services may be required to
disclose financial interests only in the construction industry.
In this case, the assumption of duties of advising an entity
on a bond issue may require the expansion of the employee's or

___________________

1/ As to whether one is a consultant within the meaning of
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(a)(2), see infra.

2/ We call this Article 2-type disclosure since it is the
same disclosure required of city councilmembers and others
whose disclosure obligations are set forth directly in Article 2
of Chapter 7 of the Act, Government Code Sections 87200 et seq.

Mr. C. Edward Dilkes                                    Page Three
November 9, 1979


or consultant's disclosure category.  At a minimum, I would
think that an appropriate disclosure category would include
underwriters and bond counsel, and depending on the purpose of
the bonds, it could include other interests as well.

   As a side note, do not be misled by the Act's exclusion
of municipal securities from the definition of "investment"
contained in Government Code Section 82034.  This means that
an official does not have to disclose his or her ownership
of such securities, but it does not mean that by participating
in decisions pertaining to such securities, he or she cannot
affect an entity which is reportable, such as a law firm, an
underwriter, or a construction company whose activities will
be financed by the bonds.

   With respect to your second question, I do not think that
bidding on a bond issue would create an additional reporting
obligation if a consultant were already required to disclose
because he or she was providing financial consulting services
to an entity.  It seems that any interests which could be affected by underwriting an issue could also be affected by virtue of
deciding whether or not to undertake the issue, and should
therefore already be contained in the consultant's disclosure
category.

   This entire discussion, however, is predicated on determining that someone is a "consultant" within the meaning of
2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18700(a)(2).  The problems with this
section are myriad, as you so aptly point out in your letter.
One of the things it does clarify, however (and perhaps the
only one) is that the term refers to an individual, a "natural
person," and not to an organization with which a governmental
entity contracts.  It is therefore the individuals who actually
provide the services for the consultant firm who must disclose
their interests, and if one individual provides engineering
services and another bond services, they should be in appropriately different disclosure categories.  With respect to the
exclusion contained in subsections (A) and (B), I can only say
that both of these requirements must be met for the exclusion
to operate.  Based on what I perceive to be the intent of the
regulation, I use the same example you did, the classic demographic study, and I routinely advise that consultants who are
performing staff functions should be treated as if they were
staff.

Mr. C. Edward Dilkes                                    Page Four
November 9, 1979


     Again, thank you for your general comments on the regu-
lation. If you would like further amplification of any of the
issues covered by this letter, please do not hesitate to call
me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Sarah T. Cameron
                                        Deputy Chief for
                                           Conflicts of Interest
                                        Legal Division

STC:nrw

TELANOFF, WALLIN, KRESS & DILKES

RONALD M. TELANOFF                   LAW OFFICES                    [illegible]
SAMUEL ROBROHSKY                     JACKSON CENTER BUILDING         [illegible]
PETER L. WALLIN                      LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90024   4703-3
C. EDWARD S. KRESS
ROBERT L. AMES
STANTON J. PAGE

October 20, 1979


Robert Stern
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 "K" Street
Sacramento, California  95814

Dear Mr. Stern:

     Our law firm represents various cities and local government
agencies.  Our clients employ a variety of "consultants"
for planning and financial matters.  In this regard, we are
unsure what status these consultants have with respect to
the Fair Political Practices Act.  Two specific questions are
posed, and we need some advice with respect to these matters.
These questions are posed at the end of the letter.

     This letter is also intended to detail a broader problem
we see in the local government sector.  Local agencies hire
"consultants" to perform a vast array of services, studies
and functions.  When a consulting firm has several different
divisions, each performing a different service, the distinction
between "conducting research" and making or influencing a
decision becomes very difficult to define.

     I personally feel the relationship of consultants to
local agencies deserves some review as a generalized problem
in local government.

I.  The Consultant in the Local Government Sector

     There are consulting firms who provide a broad range
of services.  They conduct planning studies, aid in selection
and perform engineering and architectural services.  In addition
to these capabilities, they offer services in connection with
bond issues (such as providing technical advice and assistance
to the public agency involved in order to effectuate the sale
of bonds), financial consulting, monitoring construction
activity (particularly in redevelopment projects), review of
mortgage loan for housing programs, (both new construction
and rehabilitation), program development and implementation
for local residents in redevelopment and housing projects,
assistance in preparation of financial reports, and an
advisory capacity for the investment of bond proceeds.  In

TELANOFF, WALLIN, KRESS & DIKES
LAW OFFICES

Robert Stern
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
October 20, 1979
Page Two

some instances, they wear many of these hats at the same
time.

Often there is a retainer for basic services and
an additional percentage fee in connection with bond issues.
These consultants are in a unique position to influence the
size of the bond issue involved. For example, in its
engineering, planning, and relocation capacities, the firm
determines the size of the project. Then, the financial
advisor and the consultant determine the size of the bond
issue. All of these decisions would directly affect the
size of the fees received by the consultant because, as
to this part, they are on a percentage basis.

We are also concerned about the practical situation
that is created. Some of the firms we have encountered engage
in so many functions that they effectively supplement staff
functions of the City or Agency. In these cases, the
consultant can perform all of the services normally supplied
by city staff without clear disclosure of its financial
interests. This is not a problem in any of the agencies
we represent; it does seem to be going on elsewhere.

II. 2Cal Admin. Code §18700 is not specific enough
to provide much guidance to local government
entities

With respect to "consultants" §18700 provides:

"The provisions herein define terms as
used in Chapter 7 of the Political Reform Act
of 1974, as amended, Government Code Sections
87100-87312.

"(a) 'Public official at any level of
state or government' means every natural person
who is a member, officer, employee or consultant
of a state or local government agency.

"(2) 'Consultant' shall include
any natural person who provides, under
contract, information, advice, recommen-
dation or counsel to a state or local

TELANOFF, WALLIN, KESS & DILKES
LAW OFFICES

Robert Stern
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
October 20, 1979
Page Three

government agency, provided, however,
that 'consultant' shall not include a
person who:

"(A) Conducts research and
arrives at conclusions with respect
to his or her rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel
independent of the control and
direction of the agency or of any
agency official, other than normal
contract monitoring; and

"(B) Possesses no authority
with respect to any agency decision
beyond the rendition of information,
advice, recommendation or counsel."

The inadequacy of § 18700 falls into 3 categories:

(1) Staff Functions

§ 18700 was intended to exempt consultants who
study problems but do not effect decisions. I suspect that
most consulting firms assume (in error) that they are
exempt from the disclosure requirements simply because
they are not regular city employees. This can probably be cleared
up by explaining to local entities that anyone performing
staff functions which effect decision making must be included in the local Conflict of Interest Code.

It seems to me that Section 18700 applies to a person
who only investigates and gives advice, etc. We believe
that this should be distinguished from the type of consultant
or firm, who, in essence, acts as the staff of the particular
public body involved. We believe that there is a point at
which the multiplicity of duties becomes such that a so-
called "consultant" can become staff.

Furthermore, even if the contracts are in proper form,
there may be a problem in the performance. For example,
note that some of the above items relate to the administration
of local agency funds. We believe that this should be done
by the Treasurer of the local entity under Section 11 of
Article XI of the California Constitution. However, if, in

TELANOFF, WALLIN, KRESS & GILKES
LAW OFFICES

Robert Stern
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
October 20, 1979
Page Four

actual practice, it develops that the Treasurer is merely
a rubber stamp and the real management is being done by
the so-called "consultant," the line has been crossed into
the public official category. Although we have no such
instances with any of our clients, we are informed that,
in some public agencies, this is the common practice.

(2) The definitions of exempted work (i.e.,
"Conducts Research") in Section 18700
(a)(2) are over broad.

In local government, there are two types of "studies"
which are typically performed by consultants. The regulations,
in my view correctly exempt the first type, but are not
specific enough to require compliance with the Act for the
second type.

Type I is the traditional demographic study. It makes
no recommendations for solution, or, alternatively poses
several solutions without advocating implementation of any
one solution.

Type II is a study for creation or implementation of a
program. Such a "study" may propose expenditures of vast
sums of money and ultimately have substantial impacts on the
local community. In this regard, Section 18700 is simply
inadequate. For all practical purposes, the consultant is
developing a strategy for implementation and the local entity
is inclined to rely on the report very heavily.

In fact, in Type II situations, the consultant is in a
position to create work for its other divisions (see next
subsection) or commit any of the other practices made ex-
pressly illegal by the Act itself.

For example, I am a city attorney and must disclose all
real property I own in any jurisdiction I represent. If I
were a consultant preparing a "study" for implementation of
a general plan, such disclosure is not clearly compelled by
Section 18700.

I would recommend deletion of Section 18700(a)(2)(B)
and substitute language that expressly requires consultants
preparing any proposal for implementation by the government
body to disclose.

TELANOFF, WALLIN, KRESS & DILKES
LAW OFFICES

Robert Stern
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
October 20, 1979
Page Five

     (3)  Multiple services consultants "feeding"

     If a consultant has 3 "independent" divisions or
corporations performing different functions, the power to
"feed" different parts of the same enterprise is present.

     For example, the planning division can prepare
input for a general plan which calls for expenditure of
substantial sums for engineering on public improvements and
a future bond issue to pay for the improvements.  If the
consultant wishes to perform all three services, the planning
division can be used to feed the engineering and financial
consulting divisions with future work.

     The local entity generally knows that consultant
A and consultant B are related, but rarely is this in-
formation retrievable by the public at large.  I suspect that
we would all be surprised to see the full list of related
corporate entities controlled by some of the large consulting
firms and their individual owners.

     In theory, such a consultant could also be involved
in a development company and actually "feed" that entity
as well.  In this regard, I have no concrete proof of such
practices, but the potential for abuse is present.  (I know
for a fact, such practices do not occur in our city.)

III.  Our Specific Questions

     Having expressed my over-all concern and detailed
the range of the problem (both existing and potential) my
specific questions will seem rather insignificant.  I
need to know what disclosure requirements are applicable to
consultants who:

     (1)  Advise a local entity on a bond issue while
          simultaneously performing engineering or
          planning services for the same entity.

     This problem has not confronted any of our clients
as yet, but at least one city is contemplating issuance
of housing development bonds.  In such cases, one con-
sultant typically plans and administers the program as
well as structuring the bond issuance.  The multiple

TELANOFF, WALLIN, KRESS & DILKES
LA OFFICE

Robert Stern
Chief Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
October 20, 1979
Page Six

services and feeding problems present some difficulty.

(2) Gives financial consulting services for
a bond issue and thereafter bids on the
issue.

This is a common practice engaged in by reputable
and competent firms. The intention to bid is always dis-
closed in the Official Statement and is explicitly approved
by resolution. Because bond issuance is very complex, a
local entity rarely does anything more than approve the
consultants plan. Because the act expressly exempts
municipal securities from disclosure, a change in the
regulation might not fully solve the problem.

While these specific questions are of interest
to us, the broader problem is of growing concern and
deserves substantial attention.

Very truly yours,

TELANOFF, WALLIN, KRESS & DILKES

By C. Edward Diles

CED/bs

sections.question: What disclosure requirements are applicable to consultants who: (1) advise a local entity on a bond issue while simultaneously performing engineering or planning services for the same entity; or (2) give financial consulting services for a bond issue and thereafter bid on the issue?
sections.conclusion: Consultants should generally be designated as a position with disclosure categories matching the agency head (broadest disclosure), but agency heads may make written findings to narrow disclosure categories based on limited scope of duties. For land use/planning consultants, comprehensive disclosure of investments, real property interests, and income sources is typically required. Bond advising consultants should minimally disclose underwriters and bond counsel interests. Bidding on a bond issue does not create additional disclosure obligations if the consultant already discloses due to financial consulting services. The individuals providing services (not the firm) must disclose, and they should be in appropriately different categories based on their specific duties.
sections.question_synthetic: What disclosure obligations and Conflict of Interest Code designations should apply to government consultants who perform multiple services including bond advising, engineering, planning, or bidding on bond issues?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: FPPC advises that consultants should generally receive broad disclosure requirements matching agency leadership, with case-by-case narrowing possible through written agency findings. Consultants advising on bond issues should disclose underwriter and bond counsel interests at minimum, with broader disclosure required for those involved in planning or land use decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a responsive advice letter to an October 20, 1979 incoming request letter from Dilkes. The document includes both the incoming request (pages 2-end) and FPPC's response (pages 1-2). FPPC notes ongoing internal study of consultant provisions and indicates willingness to revisit the regulation. The response provides practical guidance while acknowledging conceptual difficulties with applying the Political Reform Act to consultants.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: What disclosure requirements are applicable to consultants who: (1) advise a local entity on a bond issue while simultaneously performing engineering or planning services for the same entity; or (2) give financial consulting services for a bond issue and thereafter bid on the issue?

CONCLUSION: Consultants should generally be designated as a position with disclosure categories matching the agency head (broadest disclosure), but agency heads may make written findings to narrow disclosure categories based on limited scope of duties. For land use/planning consultants, comprehensive disclosure of investments, real property interests, and income sources is typically required. Bond advising consultants should minimally disclose underwriters and bond counsel interests. Bidding on a bond issue does not create additional disclosure obligations if the consultant already discloses due to financial consulting services. The individuals providing services (not the firm) must disclose, and they should be in appropriately different categories based on their specific duties.

embedding.summary: FPPC Deputy Chief Sarah Cameron responds to counsel C. Edward Dilkes regarding disclosure requirements for government consultants performing multiple services. The letter provides guidance on designating consultants in Conflict of Interest Codes and determining appropriate disclosure categories based on the scope and nature of their duties, particularly for those involved in bond issues, planning, and engineering services.
